CURRENT AFFAIRS,

During recent months some people of the Gaelic Lea-
gue displayed a Jamentable want of tact. After the
Procession incident tact was more than ever called for—
but, unhappily, tact has not been displayed. It will be
remembered that at a recent meeting in Dublin & charge
of attempting to damn the collection was made by one of
the speakers against us, and—so far as the report in the

 Freeman showed— his charge was permitted to be made,

~ and the speaker was not rebuked or called to order by the
hairman, who was one of the Vice-Presidents of tho
elic League ; there certainly was not much tact shown

there. The Gaclic League has an oflicial newspaper, but
the newspaper remained silent. Before Seac¢tmain
na Saeeoilge the Dublin Coiroe Ceanncain passed
the following resolution : —* That in view of the manner
in which the Post Office has endeavoured to obstruct ar-
rangements for * Seachtmhain na Gaedhilge,” and the at=
tempts from an unexpected quarter to injure the collec-
tion, we pledge ourselves to redouble our efforts to secure
most successful results.” Now, it seems to us that the
outrage on liberty committed by some apostles of free-
dom " at the Procession, though not, of course, neces-
sarily traceable to these pronouncements, was in con-
tinuity with them. The incident subsequently came up-
for discussion before this Dublin Coipoe Ceanntaip
and the following, as a result, was sent to the Press: —

“The Committee then discussed the incident which oc-
curred at the Language Demonstration, about which Dr.
MacWalter, T.C., raised a question at the Corporation
meeting of the 13th inst., and after careful consideration
adopted the following : —* While we regret that even in
one isolated instance the harmony of the Language Pro-
cession should have been. disturbed, yet as the incident
referred to was caused by persons acting without autho-
rity, and was due to the unauthorised introduction of an
individual into the section reserved for {he Corporation,
we do not consider that action on our part is called for.”
As we read this, it merely adds insult to injury. It is
only just to state that the resolution was only adopted
by a majority. We hear there was a lengthened discus-
sion over it. We do not know how many of the ten mem-
bers present belong to the Tin-Pike or King, Lords and
Commons faction of Ireland, but that faction was repre-
sented. The resolution is, indeed, a curious document.
The regret expressed, such as it is, is on account of the
harmony of the Procession having been disturbed. Now
if the editor of this paper had quietly got out of Dr.
M‘Walter’s carriage at the behest of the roughs of “free-
dom,” and if no public mention was made of the footpad
tactics, the harmony of the Procession would not have
suffered. Ts the real regret of the majority of this meet-
ing of the Coipoe Ceanntaip at the outrage committed
by the roughs, or at the consequent annoyance :\nAd want,
of harmony brought about by the p\lbhci_by'wlnch the
outrage gained? The resolution of the majority of these
ten members of the Corpoe Ceanntaip of Dublin sets
out that as the outrage was due to the unauthorised in-
troduction of an individual into the section reserved for
the Corporation * they consider that no action is called
for.’ As we read this it is really mildly condoning the
footpad tactics. The objection of the roughs was not to
an individual out of place, but to an individual ; there
would have been no objection to Mr. Gore, who also con-
stituted in himself « an unauthorised introduction of an
individual into the section reserved for the Corpora-
tion,” for Mr. Gore is not a member of the C_orp(_)mbic_m.
Suppose one of the Councillors had brought his wife with
him, and she was ordered out of the carriage asan unz
authorised individual by roughs, would the ma]_onty of
tho fen members of the Coiroe Ceanncaiq think that
no action was called for?

We turn to the official organ of last week. There is no

apology, no expression of regret. The only reference to
the blackguardly conduct of these roughs is as follows: —
«A correspondent Wwho does not give his name has writ-
ten us to inquire whether the Coisde Gnétha, through
AnClaidheamlSoluis o otherwise, will offer any apology
for an incident which occurred at the Dublin Language
Demonstration on Sunday week last. The same question
has been asked by one or two correspondents of the Irash
Independent. The answer, of course, is that the Proces-
sion was carried out under the auspices not 'of the Coisde

82 . THE LEADER.

Gnétha, but of the Dublin Coisde Ceanntair, and the
matter can come before the Coisde Gnétha only by way of
appeal from the decision of the lattec body. The Coisde

. Ceanntair has dealt with the incident in the following
resolubion which was adopted at its last meeting :—
“While we regret: that even in one isolated instance the
harmony of the Language Procession should have been
disturbed, yet, as the incident referred to was caused by
persons acting without authority, and was due to the un-
authorised introduction of an individual into the section
reserved for the Corporation, we do not consider thab
action on our part is called for.’” That is how the offi-
cial organ that in recent years cost tho Language Fund
a sum of £1,091 in subsidies deals with the matter.

Now, this sort of thing simply won’t do. The attacks
made on this paper at a meeting over which one of the
Vice-Presidents of the League presided, and the resolu-
tion passed by the Coiroe Ceanncaip previous to the
Procession are in direct continuity with the outrage.
Again the official organ has styled itself the “confrere ”
of the ex-Tin Pike, now the King, Lords and Commons of
Ireland organ. The ex-Tin Pike organ suggested that
every shilling given to the Language Fund would be a
blow at the LEapEr. Neither its  confrere,” the official
organ, nor any official of the Gaelic League repudiated
that, but instead the advertisement of SeacTmamn na
Saevilge was sent to the ex-Tin Pike organ, a “con-
frere ” of the official organ, and was ostentatiously kept
from our columns. Now the ex-Tin Pike organ, a “ con-
frere ” of the CLmveam Sotuip clapped the roughs
on the back. It will be remembered that this “ con-
frere ” of the official organ said :—“ Dr. M‘Walter, as a
member of the Corporation, was entitled personally to
participate in and attend the demonstration, but if he
refused to attend it otherwise than in the company of a
person obnoxious to Irish Ireland, there was, of course,
nothing but to bow him out.” This is an endorsement
of the conduct of the roughs, and the paper in which
this appeared has been styled a “confrere” by the
Ctardeam  Soluip No tortuous quibbling can detach
the authorities of the League from the incident. They
are mixed up in it, and it is now their duty to shake
themselves free of it. If the Coipoe Fnoca did not
expressly denounce the outrage, every thinking man
would conclude that it condoned it, and the non-political
Gaelic League would be regarded as an annexe of the
Tin Pike and King, Lords and Commons of Ireland
party.

Of course, if the Gaelic League gave reason to the
public to conclude that it was being run at headquarters
by the Tin Pike faction, whose organ clapped the act of
the roughs on the backs, that moment every branch of
the League throughout the country would have to recon-
sider its position in relation to the Central authority,
and the workmen might at once be called on to remove
the words ““ Connpao na Jaevitge” from No. 24
Upper O’Connell St., and place in their stead, for how-
ever short a period they might tarry there, “Cumman
na nSaeveal.” And after that—? We have no fear
that this deplorable situation will arise, for the Gaelic
League branches throughout the country would not stand
it. The Executive Committee, as an informal branch
of the Tin Pike Organisation, is unthinkable. We
think it fair also to ask the Coipoe Fnota to say ab
their next meeting whether or not the official organ is
to be run any further as a “confrere” of the paper that
approved the footpad tactics of the roughs of the
Procession.

The incident has created widespread indignation, but
though it may act as a temporary damper on the Gaelic

ague, we are sure that ultimately it will be proved
that the effect of the action of the roughs of the pro-
cession whose tactics have been approved by a “confrere”
of the Clarveam Soutuwp will have cleared the air.
The Gaelic League wants in many quarters levelling up.
In Dublin one need not go beyond the recont article in
the official organ itself to find reason to believe that the
League in the metropolis is in anything like a satis-
factory condition

ApriL 1, 1905.

though unhealthy, is, if we may say
so,T Eﬁtﬁgxﬁltlf(l;\’is no Zigu of inherent ill-health that a
child gets the measles, and an ugly outbreak on the skn?
of the Gaelic League is no sign of constitutional clec.;uyJ
i is matter for congratulation rather that the outbreak
occurred when the Organisation was still of tender
years. These outhreaks are more serious in old con-
stitutions; a little competent nursing and all will soon
be well.

The Irish Ireland idea was big and new; as our
readers know, we went into the desperate fight to win,
and we worked the idea with unflagging vigour. The
country rose to it, and' great, and indeed fund.zu;enba,}
changes have been brought about by the co-operation o
ourselves, our contributors and our readers. Success
attracts; and during the period when vast amounts o,i
great and more or less rough wholesome work is ab one’s
hand, there is not enough time, nor the occasion is noti
in humour, if we may put it so, for keen supervision and
careful pruning. Being an abnormal country, it 1s nfJL
surprising that abnormal fruits arose here and there.
The Irish Ireland movement, essentially common sense
and intellectual, brought to the surface some people-
whose intellect was largely domiciled in their lungs, and
whose common sense was to seek. The force was made
as a result of unflagging energy, hard’ thinking, and_ a
disregard for consequences so long as the Truth of Irish
nationality was driven full steam ahead. Many people
lacking in copious understanding, and who shouted

shibbolethes and did a hands-turn here and there, con-

scientiously thought that they were raising a great dust;
in truth the dust that was being raised half-blinded
them.

Many people have gained a passing notoriety by shout-
ing Saeveal, Saeveal, and in the stress of work their
shouts have been accepted as credentials in the great in-
tellectual, social, and industrial effort which we call the
Irish-Treland Movement.

During a temporary suspension of the operation of the
tendency towards the survival of the fittest incompetent.
persons were held in solution; we fear they are already
precipitated as sediment ; these without capacity to lead
edged their way forwards, and there were so many things
to be done that there was no time to put them back in
their places. Fortunately, at the psychological moment,
some roughs precipitated the situation, and, like a shot,
the mind of Irish Ireland was thrown upon itself, and
proceeded at once to take its soundings. The organ of
red liberty approved the roughs, who laid the services of
a policeman under contribution to force “a person ob-
noxious to Irish ITreland” out of the Language Proces-
sion, and Irish Ireland is now taking the measure of the
Tin Pike men. It was about time.

The conduct of the roughs applauded by a “confrere”
of the CLarveam Solup has cleared the air. We
urge that the next Parliament of the Gaclic League be
open to the public; we urge that a report and accounts
be submitted to the public before the meeting of the &po
Feip; we urge that every person desiving to se:ve as
an elected member of the Cotpoe Snota be compelled
to state his views and policy. If 'he believes that in the
name of liberby men who have spent years in the attempt
to create an Irish Ireland should be treated to footpad
tactics let him say so, and if the constituent elements of
the Qjvo-Feip agree with him they will place a man
with such a programme ab the head of the poll ; if they
disapprove of that interpretation of:the spirit of immor-
tal liberty they will, on the other hand, place him at
the bottom of the poll. But let us, in the name of com-
mon sense and representative institutions, have no more
Lxecutives clected on the pig-in-a-poke system. Prac-
tical men may discount pre-election promises, but yet
they aro some check. Before any member is elected’ to
the next Executive to be formed in August wo demand,
and we write in the spirit of democracy and of Tepresen-
tabive assemblies of the progressive world, thab they first
state their views on all the chief points of policy concern-
ing the Gaelic League Organisation. We trust the

people when the people are trusted. We will respect,
any Executive of the Gaelic League elected freely,
openly, and in conformity with the usages of modern
democracy. When the roughs applauded by the ex-Tin
Pike organ held us up, we think, to use a memorable
phrase -gf Father Yorke’s,  they struck the wrong pro-
cession.

Our contemporary, the Cork Sun, of March 26th, has
a short editorial note headed “ Pressmen and Roughs."”
It runs as follows:—“ We are glad to sce that Gaclic
Leaguers and fair-minded Irishmen generally condemn
the treatment given to the Editor of the Dublin Lraper
_ab the Language Procession in Dublin. The Editor of
the LEADER was the pioneer of the Irish Ireland Move-
ment when the ‘patriots’ who interfered with him were
densgly ignorant as to what the Revival meant. The
" Gaelic Lague has done the right thing in disowning and
disassociating themselves from those who took on them-
selves the task of ‘ firing out’ the man to whom is to be
« credited the success of the Irish Revival.” Our contem-
porary, no doubt wrote the above note before the
majority among ten members of the Dublin Coipoe
Ceannct a1 passed their absurd resolution, and before
the last issue of the official organ appeared. A proper
repudiation and adequate apology have not yet been
forthcoming. 3

Our contemporary, the Lwvening News, of Waterford,
prints a long letter on the LEADER incident in its issuc
of March 20th from “an old Gaelic Leaguer.” The let-
ter winds up as follows:—* Finally, if the Executive
cannot see their way to have this given to the Editor of
the LEADER, who has done more for the uplifting of Ire-
land from the quagmire of West Britishism than any
man in the ranks of the Irish Revival Movement at the
present day, I, as well as many other Gaelic Leaguers,
will never recognise the Dublin Executive, as represent-
ing the spirit of the Gaelic Movement.” Our Waterford
contemporary has some editorial notes on the incident
also. The concluding note is as follows:—To give the
Editor of the LEADER his due, it cannot be denied that it
was he who, in a great measure, did a lot of pioneer work
in preparing the way for present-day Leaguers, and with-
out him the demonstrations held in the different parts of
the country would not have been.”

In the course of a letter to us from the Secretary of
a Southern Branch of the Gaelic League, our corres-
pondent says:—“It is with pleasure I notice you are
not alone in the fight with the ‘clique’ in the Gaelic
League. As a Gaelic Leaguer, I endorse all you did.
Indeed, the Gaelic League would be but a myth but for
your advocacy.”

. We.see in the last issue of our contemporary the
Anglo Celt, that at a meeting of the Committee of the
Cavan branch of the Gaelic League, it was unanimously
resolved that the Secretary be directed to enquire from
the Central Executive whether any steps had been,
or are intended to be taken, to repudiate the action of
the persons who attacked the editor of the LeApER dur-
ing the Language Procession.

A correspondent urging the necessity of a DaiLy
‘LeaDER writes:— The ruffianly outrage committed
on you, and through you, on all Leaderites only emphi-
sises the necessity of such a start.”

A reverend correspondent, in the course of a letter to
us, writes:—“T need not, say that the blood b91lcd in
my veins when I heard of the disgraceful and discredit-
able incident, and I'd have written you and the Clai-

Dearh ab once, but I learned from one of the Coipoe
Bnota that an apology would be immediately tendered.
All this is a loud, though involuntary, testimony to your
worth, and sure 'tis God’s truth that if we are not afraid
to speak out to the enemies of the country it’s you who
taught us to speak. God strongthen your arm, and

cep your big heart full of hope. Everyone I talked to

THE LEADER. £

of the incident condemned the action, and expressed in-
tense indignation as well as wholehearted sympathy for
yourself.”

Tho exorbitant police tax was discussed recently’ &t
the Corporation of Dublin, and we hope the Corporation
will stick to the matter, and use to the full whatever
powers they have, particularly with a view to obtaining
control of the city police force. By the way, we note
that the ox-Tin Pike organ heads an article in its last
issue— Why pay the police tax?” One reason, though
it may be an inadequate one, is that the services of a
D.M.P. may sometimes come in handy in the way of
helping to move on an “obnoxious person ” at the behest
of o crowd of rowdy “rebels.” Oh, liberty! into what
an angel of green, immortal Trish freedom thy name may
transform a liveried champion of the base and brutal
British power! In the course of the article in the ex-
Tin Pike organ the following question is asked : — Why
should we pay bhis police tax to support a standing army
which terrorises us, which is a sink of most wasteful ex-
penditure, and over which we have no control?” Now, is
it absolutely true that “ we” have no control over the
police? Can roughs who were able to secure the services
of a D.M.P. on a recent occasion be said to have “no
control ” over these “ minions” of the British Govern-
ment? In the course of the same article we further
read : — Unlike the cost of the Constabulary, the price
of the precious ‘ protection’ of the police in Dublin is
paid for directly from the pockets of the citizens. And
yet who dare rise in his seat in the City Council and
move a censure on the meanest police official for any
crime whatever?’ Well, the roughs, on a recent occa-
sion, got some value for their money. Might we suggest
that some member should move a vote of thanks to the
D.M.P. whose services were recently placed at the dis-
posal of the roughs of “immortal liberty?”

The “ New Rule ” of the “ National ” Board of Educa-
tion came in for some vigorous treatment at the hands of

84

the Very Rev. Monsignor Hallinan at the recent meeting
of the priests and teachers of the Deanery of Newcastle

* “West. This rule, as all our readers are probably aware,
is as follows:—Rule 127 (b)—“ Boys under cight years
of age are ineligible for enrolment in a boys’ school where
there is not an assistant mistress, unless there is no suit-
able school under a mistress available in the locality.”
In the course of Monsignor Hallinan's vigorous on-
slaught on this innovation, he said :—“ But apart from
this, the rule would lead to the dismissal of half the
male assistants in the country! Already the female as-
sistants are more than double the number of male assis-
tants, for, according to the recent Report of the Com-
missioners, the number of assistant teachers in National
Schools in Ireland is 3,387, of whom 2,699 are women,
and only 1,088 men. So far as you-and I can forecast,
if this new rule were put into operation half these latter
assistants would have to leave and be cast on the world
—some of them married men with families—many of
them trained teachers representing practically, from a
financial point of view, a loss of £100 each to the coun-
¢ry, and an incalculable educational loss.” After dis-
cussing the probable effects of the carrying out of this
rule in the teachers’ prospects, Monsignor Hallinan went
on :—“ Now, if you apply these figures to the schools in
this Deanery alone, you can see what havoc and ruin
it means for many deserving teachers—what degradation
for others! And the Deanery is typical of all Ireland.
Are we, the Clerical Managers of Ireland, to whom these
worthy men look for protection, going to abandon them
in their time of need? Are we going to allow them to
be evicted out of our schools at the bidding of any Trea-
sury officials or Education Board? Most decidedly not.
Will the people of this nation—the public boards 1n Ire-
land—our Parliamentary representatives—look on with
indifference at this iniquitous eviction of a most deserv-
ing body of public servants? Certainly not! Will the
inale teachers themselves tolerate it? They would be
unworthy of the name of Irishmen if they did not rise up
as one man in opposition to it. Will the female teachers
of Ireland, in whose interests the new policy is supposed
to work, co-operate with the authors of this absurd and
obnoxious rule? They are not here to answer, but I'll
answer for them. The female teachers of Ireland have
no sympathy with, nor will they give any support to, a
policy which means ruin and degradation for their fellow-
teachers—many of whom are their own fathers, brothers,
or sons.” In a brilliant sentence Monsignor Hallinan
summed up his view of the objects aimed at by the
authors of this “ New Rule.” He said—“It is cheap
labour at the expense of educational progress.”

Referring to the rule bearing on the special fees for
extra subjects—especially Irish—Monsignor THallinan
said :—“ I venture to propose a better one, which, in-
stead of putting back the hands of the clock, will be the
natural outcome of the progressive policy hitherto pur-
sued by the Commissioners, and will be, I have no doubt.
accepted with gratitude by the country at large. Let
them continue to spoon-feed the Irish language yet
awhile by special fees—vary somewhat in the distribu-
tion of these special grants—and let them at once boldly
declare that at the end of, say three or five years, there
will be no special fees paid for the Irish language, but
that thenceforward it will be a compulsory subject in
every National School. By doing so the Training Col-
leges will have to fall into line more cordially than they
have hitherto done; the teaching of Irish will neces-
sarily become compulsory in them; Summer Training
Colleges will be multiplied; centres will be formed
through the country .where the teachers themselves can
learn. In a word, at the end of five years the machinery
would be ready for the enforcement of compulsory in-

. struction in Irish in the National Schools. By doing
this the Commissioners of National Education will be
relieved from the lecturing and hectoring of Treasury
officials, and will earn for themselves the undying grati-
tude of the Irish race.” .

cannot but envy the changes wrought by your journal in

Now it appears that the C Division has been notorious
for the bigotry operating within it for some years. The
Protestants arein the proportion of one to six Catholics
in tho C Division. In this division there are about forty
special duty posts—these mean a short tour and less
night duty than the ordinary tour. Now we are in-
formed that all the ‘ saved” couls(;ab.lcs in the C Division
avo allotted one of those special jobs. It cannot be
claimed that “saved ” constables are superior in intelli-
cence to the “Idolators,” The Catholic constable
has to perform every second month one night
duty with extras, which means that he must do
from 10 p.m. to 6 a.m. for twenbysix nights of his
month on night duty, although the Act of I"ﬂ’rl'mmenb
only prescribes sixteen long nights at most. rh.‘.s work
is thrown on the Catholic constables, and so the “saved

constables are allotted the special posts and day duty.

The latest additions to the G Division, the staff ser-
geants of the A and D Divisions, the Prisons Board and
Telegraph Staff at the Castle, are interesting. Various
Tdolator applicants sought for these positions, but they
had no chance of getting them whatever their qualifica-
tions.

“Saved ” members of the C Division, who belong to
the Green Street Barracks Sub-division of Store St., are

Referring to the publicans and St. Patrick’s Day,
our contemporary the Leinster Leader says:—“It is our
painful task to remark that, notwithstanding the noble
efforts of our Bishops and priests, as well as of the Gaelic
League, to have the National Festival observed in a
reverential and self-respecting manner, it was only too
apparent in many parts of Ireland that the “back doors”
of a large number of the public-houses admitted flocks
of men and women who could not resist the strong temp-
tation thus afforded for drowning the Shamrock. We
must say that the publicans who promised to close their
establishments for the day, and who put up their
shutters, while at the same time their doors were ajar to
admit all comers, were guilty of very reprehensible and
retrogressive conduct.” And again : —“The pitiable and
objectionable spectacle of men and—we regret to say
it—women too, staggering out of “closed” public-houses,
meb the gaze in many quarters; and the poor tradesmen
and their wives were afforded the opportunity of spend-
ing on maddening liquor the money which was sorely
needed for the clothing and maintenance of their
unfortunate children.” The publicans who opened, and
those who did a “‘back door” trade on St. Patwick’s Day,
no doubt, by sponging on St. Patrick, put extra money
in their tills, but ultimately they will find, we believe,
that they have only strengthened the volume of opinion
that is shocked at the nation-ruiners who soak upwards
of £14,000,000 per annum from this poor country. As
nation-killers, what are the landlords now to the
Bungs!

Father Murphy, M.S., the energetic Irish Ireland
priest of Enniscorthy delivered a lecture in the Town
Hall Loughrea, recently, under the auspices of the local
Total Abstinence Society. His Lordship the Most Rev.
Dr. O'Dea, presided, and in putting the vote of thanks
to the lecturer, said, according to a report in a local
paper :—“Dealing with the drink evil his Lordship said
that the annual drink bill for Ireland was 14 millions
of money, and seeing some time ago some statistics
quoted by the Bishop of Limerick as to the amount of
money spent in that city for drink, it struck him thab
it would be interesting to find out the amount spent in
his own cathedral town. From data supplied by a
friend he found that for the months of December,
January, and February last, they had spent for porter
alone, £2,900—or practically £3,000. He did not by
any means, however, say that this amount was spent in
the town alone. The sum mentioned representied the
amount expended in a district embracing something like
a radius of 6 or 7 miles. If the months of December,
January, and February might be taken as a fair aver-

was £11,600. Their whiskey bill added to that brought

The question of the attendance of workhouse childrer:
ab public outside schools is an important one. We do'
not think that anyone with a clear head and a Christian
heart would disagree with the proposition that every
child who has the misfortune to be in a workhouse ougl'm
to receive his education amongst the boys and girls in
the country’s schools who have the fortune to be outside
the workhouse. Itis not the fault of poor little children
that they are in the workhouse, and the unfortunate lot;
to which they ave born ought, from every point of view,
and particularly from the Christian point of view, be
made as light and hopeful as possible. The practice of
sending workhouse children to outside schools is fairly
common, and we think that every right-thinking man'
would wish it to be universal. Recently there was a re-
actionary motion to withdraw certain workhouse children
from the convent schools, and revert to the old system of
having a house teacher appointed. Monsignor Halli-
nan, P.P., of Newcastle West, happily threw in his
weight against this reactionary proposal, and so the chil-
dren are still being sent to the convent schools. As Mon-
signor Hallinan, in a letter to one of the Guardians,
said : —** Everybody who has a thought or a desire for
the welfare of these little children, and practical know-
ledge of a workhouse and its surroundings, must feel that,
it is a blessing to them to be taken away as much as pos~
sible from its prison-like, depressing and demoralising’
atmospheres, and have them brought out into the open
and into contact with other children. Hence it was only
natural that you, as their Guardians, whose primary con-
cern is their material and moral well-being, should take
that step. You are not, however, the first Board of
Guardians in Ireland that has done so. I wish you were,
and had done so years ago, for nothing but credit is due
to those Guardians who initiated this progressive policy."”
We hope that Monsignor Hallinan’s prediction will come
true that— For myself I am convinced that within a
dozen years you will have very few unions in Ireland
keeping on the old system, which is self-condemned, and
ought to be discarded as soon as possible everywhere.”

We are glad to be able to announce that the Munster
Feis, 1905, will be held on the 6th, 7th, 8th, 9th, and
10th of August next. On the opening day, Sunday,
there will be a band promenade, at which one of the most:
celebrated of our South of Ireland bands will be asked
to perform. Music of a distinctly Irish character, in-
cluding the best productions of our modern Irish com-
posers, will be rendered. On Monday, the 7th August,
the Munster Conference will be held.  Even at this early
date motions to be brought forward at this conference
will be accepted and filed, and it is to be hoped that by
the middle of next July a goodly number will be ou
hands for the printer. ~ On Tuesday and Wednesday
there will be the Competitions, and, from what we have
seen of the syllabus, it will not be the fault of the Com-
mittee if they do not attract crowds of competitors from
every part of Munster. A Grand Turus will be held ox
Thursday, the 10th August, and opportunities for a
cheap and enjoyable trip will be placed at the disposal of
all Gaelic League visitors. Munster Gaels are invited
to consider the feasibility of making a portion of their
summer holidays coincide with the Munster Feis week
next August in Killarney.

Belfast is often held up as a sort of model to Ireland.
There was an action recently tried in the Courts there
before Mr. Justice Kenny, and his Lordship, addressing
the Jury, said:—“He had to confess that in the pre-
sent case, having regard to the number of witnesses
examined on each side, and the point-blank assertions
on the one side that were denied upon the other, he
felt the greatest difficulty in conveying to the jury what
the indication of his mind was—if his mind was made
up at all—he meant as a judge of fact. Five witnesses
for plaintiff, and six for defendants, had point-blank
denied what, was asserted by each side. He had rarel
tried a case, though he had tried a fair number in Bel-
fast and also in Dublin, where there was such contra-
dictory evidence. He certainly had never come across
a case where there was such point-blank swearing, as in
this case. As a general rule he could get some indica-

THE LEADER. 87

GOYERNMENT BOARD OFFICIALS—HOW
APPOINTED—THESR QUALIFICATIONS ?

[ N the last issue I dealt in a general way with the

unsympathetic and unfriendly feelings displayed by
the Irish Government Boards to the locally elected
Boards where any opportunity occurred, and very
often when the opportunity did not offer, some under-
strapper or principal clerk took good care to male one.
In a brief way we contrasted the manner in which
Lnglish Boards treat the elected bodies in that country,
straining a point to meet their views, if it can possibly
be done, or giving kindly notice or a friendly warning,
if necessary, before any extreme measure of authority
is exercised. Arguing in fact where they might order,
and in small insignificant matters shutting their official
eyes, to the little lapses, and small peccadilloes, of the
elected Councils, or Corporations. In the one case we
have the official authorities in sympathy with the
people and the representatives they elect, and a mutual
feeling of give and take between them; a desire o
transact the business of the Boards in the spirit, at least,
if not in the absolute letter of the regulations. Now con-
trast it with the other. In our Irish unrepresentative
Government, Boards—costly and inefficient as they are,
and that phase of them we shall deal with later on—
one would imagine they would steer clear of conflict, and
undue exercise of their authoritive powers, that they
would, knowing the circumstances and the conditions
under which they are appointed, trembling as they now
are on the brink of Home Rule, they would not go out of
their way to unduly interfere with the locally elected
Boards, and worry and annoy those bodies who give
their time and their intelligence gratis—very often to
do unpleasant and disagreeable duties. Yet this is the
very policy they adopt, because the tradition in every
Irish Government Department is the spirit begotten of
ascendancy, the arrogance and insolence of office, and
the affected superior knowledge that intolerance begets.
In the past it was the rule and the custom, and now,
though the times have changed, and that we are on the
brick of still greater changes, they cannot forego or for-
get, or even modify it.

We are glad to see the “ Public Bodies’ Order ” has at,
last—so over-reaching is it in its demands, and so
costly and useless, even if it ever came to be applied—
so straitened and stiffened the backs of the Councils and
the Corporations, and put them on their mettle and in
such a fighting mood, that the Local Government Board
would be glad it had never raised such a hornet’s nest
about its ears. So far as we know, not a single one of
the elected Boards—the loyal and disloyal alike—has
said a good word in favour of the attempted ukase,
and we know if the Local Government Board could
decently retreat from their untenable position, with
regard to this measure, they would, and in fact they
will have to do so. They are already climbing down
where any approach is being made to them by any of
the locally elected bodies to modify the order, and they
will have to climb down lower still and retrace their
steps and let matters locally remain as they are, or we
are no judge of the situation. = I am pleased to know my
comments on this matter have induced the “General
Council of the County Councils” to meet—as I write
the date is not fixed—and I have no doubt that repre-
sentative body will deal with this autocratic and useless
display of power by the Local Government Board, in a
drastic and forcible manner. :

I have, perhaps, too emphatically pressed the point
with regard to this “Public Bodies’ Order” of the Local
Government Board, but that Board is so persistent in
its attitude of opposition to the locally elected bodies,
and it has so many opportunities of displaying its
powers, and so seldom fails to avail itself of its oppor-
tunities in that way, that I wish to push home the
point, more especially as in this instance the Board:
has taken up such an untenable position. No one com-

KINCORA.

FTER the shallow cynicism of the “Well of Saints,”
A Lady Gregory’s “ Kincora” came as a pleasant
and wholesome change at the Abbey Theatre. “ Kin-
cora is the vigorous product of a healthy mind that has
an appreciation for art, and yet is not out of touch with
reality. The first strictly historical drama (if I recollect
aright) produced by this society, it was an unquestioned
success, and seems to open out a new path on which our
dramatist may tread with safety and success. The
audience soon showed that it appreciated the purer
atmosphere of the drama—an atmosphere neither
dimmed by mist, nor tainted by foul vapours, and there
was far more real enthusiasm shown than I have lately
seen ab any of this society’s productions.

The play deals with the life of the great Brian of the
Tributes, and is intended to present, in a striking form,
his glory as a warrior, but still more his work as a
bringer of peace and unity to Ireland. Over against
him is set as a foil his Danish queen, Gormleith—ruin
of men, of ships, and of cities, one of those Irish Helens
of whom we have known three or four in our history.
In the first act we meet Brian at Kincora, seeking to
bring about a general peace, only to be frustrated in his
efforts by Gormleith. In the second we see him once
again. That which was not to be achieved by peace
he has gained in war. Brian, another Agamemnon,
returns in the glory of victory to his palace laden with.
the spoils of his conquest; but yet, in a fine scene, he
rejects the counsel of cruelty, and spares his enemies.
The high-kingship falls to his lot, and Gormleith be-

in secret letters she summons her Danish kin to war.
Clontarf and the death in victory of the Irish hero
are the sequel. :

These are but the main lines of the play. The action
is stayed now and then by quaint and humorous scenes
between the king’s servants after the manner of
Shakspere, and there are also two scenes where a woman
of the Sidhe is introduced as tempting Brian and his

the Sidhe. One might possibly construe these scenes
as a satire on some of the authors of the literary theatre,
bub the general view was that they were somewhat of an
excrescence, and had better be left out of the drama.

The play is really made up of two parts—Brian and
Gormleith.  Both are strongly and sympathetically
drawn, but Lady Gregory has shown herself merciless in
her delineation of the female character. Brian is with
Lady Gregory what Henry V. is with Shakspere—the
mighty warrior and the perfect man ; the great national
and Ohristian hero. Iis character is idealised through-
out; so much idealised, indeed, that the student of his-
tory may, perhaps, find himself a little perplexed. With
Brian Eire, as a whole, is everything; he fights but
that he may sheathe his sword; and his kingship is to
be the reign of peace, of mercy, and of union. XHis
heart, is loving, his trust in God is strong, and he dies
i’}l the day of his country’s victory with a psalm upon his
ips.

Gormleith, the foreign queen, knows not the gospel of
peace. Surpassing fair, in all outward seeming, she has
stored up within her the forces of disunion and destruc-
tion. The lust of battle alone appeals to her, and she
despises Brian when he thinks of rest. Stately in her
mien, she is fierce and bloody in her passions, loved of
all men, and loving Brian, yet ready to destroy him.

The acting of Mr. F. J. Fay as Brian, and of Miss Nic
Shiubhlaigh as Gormleith, was excellent. Mr. Fay was
a noble Brian, and he spoke his exalted words with a
kingly grandeur worthy of Robertson ; and yet there was
also a gentle naturalness in his tones  Miss Nic Shiubh-
laigh enacted the part of Gormleith with stately distinc-
tion; she seemed in very truth a queen, even though a
bad one. In the one emotional scene, however, where
Gormleith hesitates to sign the letters that betray Brian,
I thought her less successful ; she remained too much the
tragedy queen, and did not let the woman show through.
Mr. A. Power as Malachy, though he acted the more
emotional scenes fairly well, was utterly lacking in dig-
nity, and had not got his body under control, %

method of stringing together a number of historical
scenes, which have only a limited unity—a unity rather
of character, or of results than of plot and incident.

THE CATHOLIC TRUTH SOCIETY OF IRELAND.

7 HERE is dissatisfaction in the country at the

manner in which the Catholic Truth Society is
worked—or, perhaps it would be more correct to say, not
worked. The Society was started about five years ago.
The Irish Bishops not only gave it their approbation, but
it was advocated in many Pastorals. Branches were es-
tablished in every diocese, and these not only in large
towns but even in remote districts.  Catholic Truth
boxes were to be seen in almost every church ; the publi-
cations were largely bought up. In the cowrse of the in-
augural address—in November, 1899—of the president,
‘the present Archbishop of Tuam, his Grace said : — The
main object of the Society is, as its name implies, the
diffusion, by means of cheap publications, of sound Cath-
olic literature in popular form, so as to give instruction
and edification in a manner most likely to interest and
attract the general reader. It is well known that various
printing presses in Great Britain daily pour out a flood of
infidel and immoral publications, some of which over-
flows to this country. We have a confident hope that
the Society’s publications will remove the temptation of
having recourse to such filthy garbage, will create a taste
for a pure and wholesome literature, and will also serve
as an antidote against the poison of dangerous or im-
amoral writings.”

Notwithstanding that the publications set out in the
first catalogue were chiefly composed of reprints of his-
torical, devotional and other works familiar to an older
generation, they were new to many. The Society owed its
immediate origin to a paper read by the Rev. Dr. O'Rior-
dan, of Limerick, at the Maynooth Union of 1899, and
it was at once successful beyond all expectations. But
societies must march forward with the times; a constant
supply of new and original works is necessary, and if
they are not procured if; is only fair to ask the manage-
ment the reason why. As it is we hear that the boxes
in many parishes have been closed. In a paper read by
Mr. R.J. Kelly, B.L., at the last Conference, he said : —
“Thus the boxes were in many places not kept up to
-date—not filled with fresh supplies of books—and in too
many cases they were allowed to become the receptacle
for dog-eared, weather-beaten, dirt-covered books, that
only repelled the public. And so, in too many instances
that came under my own observation even, have the
hoxes so kept become the means of inducing the public
to believe the Society was dead, or if not dead, in so
‘moribund a state that it cannot supply books any
Tonger.” It is all very well to blame the boxes. But is the
-state of some of these boxes a leading cause of the state
.of affairs of the Catholic Truth Society of Ireland? Does
not the great responsibility for the existing state of
‘things rest with the committee of management in not

THE ORANGE “INVINCIBLES.”

N the 6th May, 1882, the Irish Chief Secretary and
the Under-Secretary were stabbed to death in the
Pheenix Park. The crime sent a thrill of horror through-
out Ireland and Great Britain, and, indeed, throughout
the “ civilized world,” if one may use that expression
without infringing copyright. The assassins were held
up to execration, and the whole character of the nation
was deemed disgraced by the dreadful deed. Despite the
efforts of the party of Law and Order, however, it was
found impossible to implicate the leaders of the National
Movement, in the crime. The representatives of Law
and Order, though relying on forgers, perjurers, and
spies as their allies, and though trying their case before
a prejudiced British tribunal, failed to prove that their
opponents had expressed a post hoc sympathy with the
murderers.  These wretched men received sympathy
from no quarter; deserved none. Yet it should be
borne in mind that what they did, they did for no per-
sonal end. They had no private grudge against the men
they slew. They did not even know them by sight. They
had nothing individually to gain by their destruction.
At @ time when agrarian strife was at its height the
moonlighter who shot his landlord from behind a hedge
had at least the satisfaction of vevenge. The Invincibles
wero city men; the wrongs of the tenants were abstract
questions for them. A mistaken idea of patriotism led
them to act as they did, and in due course they suffered
for their crime; the basest among them perished for his
perfidy.

% THE LEADER.

In the year 1905 another Irish Chief Secretary has
been laid low, and the Under-Secretary has had a hard
fight, to preserve his position. The attackers this time
have nob resorted to steel, they have not dyed their
hands with the blood of their victims.  They have
broken no civil law. They are not criminals. But they
have sought as eagerly, and in one case they have suc-
ceeded as completely as did the men of 82 in destroying
the official lives of those to whom they were opposed.
Mr. Wyndham, as an Irish Chief Secretary, is as dead
as Lord Frederick Cavendish; Sir Antony McDonnell,
for aught the public really knows, may lie, so far as his
career in Ireland is concerned, as near the point of dis-
solution as did Mr. Burke on that May evening long ago.
The Invincibles of '82 were, for the most part, ignorant
men possessed of neither place nor power, in blind hatred
of oppression and misrule, which, maybe they but im-
perfectly understood the cause of, but of whose effects
they were well aware, they endangered body and soul,
and took two fellow-beings’ lives. The Ulster Party of
to-day boast of their education, and insist on being
maintained in the place and power they now enjoy. The
fear that they may not be so preserved is the motive that
has impelled them in seeking the downfall of the two
Secretaries. Selfishness—a personal feeling of each one
for himself and for his class—is the keynote to the atbi-
tude of the Loyal Unionist.

The downfall of Mr. Wyndham has the peculiarity
of being regarded as a triumph by Unionist and Nation-
alist alike. There has seldom been a case where more
opposite Teasons have been assigned for one happening.
Some Unionists there are who pity him, because his ‘“ col-
league ” deceived him. They urge that Mr. Wynd-
ham was not to blame. He never really meant to im-
prove the methods of Dublin Castle. He had no serious
intention of affording Catholics a proper system of
Higher Education. It was not part of a programme
which he purposed to put into force that the Ascendancy
Party should be made forego the position they have held
so long. His mistake lay in permitting a subordinate
animated by these reactionary sentiments to assume too
large a share in the government of Ireland.

The Freeman's Journal and the school of politicians,
of which it is the organ, agree to some extent with these
views. They maintain that the ex-Chief Secretary
affected a sympathetic attitude solely to mislead and dis-
arm the Irish people, and treat the Under-Secretary as
the ¢gnis fatuus he employed to lead them into the
morass. On the other hand, some Nationalist Free-
thinkers—if they may be so-called—incline sympatheti-
cally towards Mr. Wyndham. They credit him with
good intentions which, in this instance, have paved the
way to rather than the floor of the abode of the ruined
statesman. So do those Irish Unionists who have sent
him thither. ‘“We are Irishmen,” they cry, and one
feels tempted to reply in a paraphrase of Macbeth, “Aye,

in the catalogue ye pass for Irishmen.”

AN IRISH UMBRELLA-MAKING FIRM.

T is easy to estimate that a large sum of money leaks

out of Ireland through the purchase of imported
umbrellas. Our uncertain climate suggests a big mar-
ket, for such articles of which market British manufac-
turers take the lion’s share.  Ireland, however, has
umbrella manufacturers whose hold on the home market
is increasing. Much of the raw material, so to speak.
used by the latter has of necessity to be brought into the
country, but its manufacture into the finished product
represents an Irish industry which is worthy of whole-
hearted support. The writer has received a whole-
sale catalogue of one of these firms, Messrs. Francis
Smyth and Son, whose workshops are situated in their
premises, 75  Grafton Street, Dublin. It is
a neatly got up publication, and comprises a long list
of the articles made by the firm as well as
various items such as walking-sticks, Irish blackthorns,
etc., in which they also deal. The cover contains im-
prints of medals awarded for excellence of merit at
different Irish Exhibitions since 1850 and several foot-
notes emphasise that the umbrellas sold by the firm are
guaranteed to be of their own manufacture. At present
the firm do a wholesale and retail business, having
branches in Dublin besides the Grafton Street house.
The wholesale department is a comparatively recent
addition. ~Many years ago they carried on a large
wholesale business, but lack of support induced them
to discontinue it and concentrate their attention on the
retail trade. Bub in the Irish Industrial Revival, the
proprietors saw an opportunity which they determined
not to ignore, and, accordingly, in 1902, they re-opened
in the wholesale line with successful results.

THE “BOND SLAYE OF BUNG.”

Sir,—I have read “:€ipc ” remarks in your last issue
on “Bung and His Bond Slave ” with much interest. It
would be well if a few of the disillusions of the “ bar”
business were better known to parents who contemplate
sending their children to drunkeries in the city or coun-
try. Itis due to the ignorance of parents to send their
sons to a business in which the working hours are long-
and slavish, and the scale of pay bad.

The average salary for an assistant may be found be-
tween the figures of £18 and £25 per annum indoor.
This wage is entirely disproportionate to the labour, and
to the long business hours. We all know that “ Bung”
opens at 7 a.m. and closes at 11 p.m.—sixteen hours a
day! But the work of the “ bond slave ” is by no means
ended even then; for the bar has to be cleared up, shop
swept, etc.; and I know houses where the assistants, or
apprentices and assistants, have to do the bottling of*
stout or ale after closing time. And then there is the
work on the Sabbath, two till seven, and the “bona fides”
as well. Out of these one hundred and one hours of’
white slavery per week, how many hours does “Bung”
give his assistants for recreation? It may come as a swr-
prise to the public to know that the usual custom, or
rule, is three hours off one night in the week; usually
8 p.m. to 11 p.m. It is worth recording this as an in-
stance of the generous magnanimity of “Bung.”

A young man has absolutely no opportunity of better-
ing himself mentally or physically with three hours only
off per week. He cannot attend a Gaelic League class or
social club of any sort.

And the moral aspect of the business has also to be con-
sidered. Because a public-house is a public-house ; it is
in it all sorts of petty scandals are openly discussed,
and coarse jests and “tips” bantered freely about, to:
the complete demoralisation of the young fellows behind
the bar.

Of course, I do not mean it to be understood that all
the grievances I speak of exist in all the licensed houses
in the trade. On the contrary, there are houses that
are well kept, and where the assistants are well and
decently treated. These houses though, are “few and
fav between ;" they do not, I fear, compose one-fourth
of the licensed houses in the country. And what pros-
pects has a young man entering this business, with its
bad pay, bad housing, and long hours. Does “ Bung ”
usually bring up his children to the trade? Not he.
He makes professional men of them, or tries to.

Now that the “Shop-Hours Act” is extending itself
to this country, it is the duly of the “Bond Slaves of
Bung " to get the voice of public opinion to put an end
to the long hours that exist in public houses.
